items.correct: Bundling data and methods together while restricting direct access to internal state.
items.random_wrong: Encrypting data so it cannot be read.
items.why: Students take 'capsule' too literally. Encapsulation combines two ideas: keeping data and behaviour TOGETHER, and HIDING implementation behind a public interface.

items.correct: The ability of different classes to respond to the same method call in their own way.
items.misconception: Having multiple classes with the same name.
items.random_wrong: Variables that change type at runtime.
items.why: Students confuse polymorphism with naming. Polymorphism means 'many forms' - a single interface (method signature) behaving differently depending on the object's actual class.

items.q: What does inheritance allow a subclass to do?
items.correct: Reuse and extend the attributes and methods of its superclass.
items.random_wrong: Copy code from any unrelated class.
items.why: Students think inheritance overrides everything. It actually REUSES the parent's members (which can be added to or overridden), creating an 'is-a' relationship.

items.q: Hiding internal state behind methods is called:
items.why: Correct answer: Encapsulation. “Inheritance” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: An object is best described as:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: An instance of a class. “A class blueprint” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many objects can be created from a single class (in principle)?
items.why: The real figure is 1000000. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
